1:
Ὦ ἀνόητοι Γαλάται (anoētoi) — "O foolish Galatians!" Anoētos (not using the mind, lacking understanding) — a censure of intellectual failure rather than moral failure; the Galatians are not wicked but foolish. This word differs from the term used for moral fools in Matthew 7:26 or 1 Cor 1:20; Paul charges not wickedness but failure to think through what they have experienced.

τίς ὑμᾶς ἐβάσκανεν — "who has bewitched you?" Baskaino (to bewitch, cast the evil eye, fascinate) — the only NT use of this term; it implies a malevolent external force has distorted their perception. οἷς κατ᾽ ὀφθαλμοὺς Ἰησοῦς Χριστὸς προεγράφη ἐσταυρωμένος — "before your very eyes Jesus Christ was publicly portrayed as crucified." Prographō (to write publicly, post, placard) — a public announcement posted for all to see; Paul's preaching of the crucified Christ was as vivid and public as a civic proclamation in the agora.
2: ἐξ ἀκοῆς πίστεως ἢ ἐξ ἔργων νόμου (akoēs pisteōs) — "from the hearing of faith or from the works of the law?" Akoē pisteōs can be read as (a) "the hearing that consists in faith" (faith-hearing, i.e., the act of receiving the message with trust) or (b) "the message that calls forth faith" (the faith-producing proclamation). Either reading makes the point: the Spirit arrived through the act of hearing and trusting the gospel, not through law-observance. Paul calls this a single argument sufficient to decide the case — the Galatians know how they received the Spirit, and law-keeping had nothing to do with it.
3: ἐναρξάμενοι πνεύματι νῦν σαρκὶ ἐπιτελεῖσθε — "having begun by the Spirit, are you now being completed by the flesh?" Enarchomai (to begin, make a start) and epiteleō (to complete, bring to full realization, perfect) frame the question as one of completion: the Spirit inaugurated the Galatians' Christian life; to seek law-keeping as its completion is to seek a fleshly fulfillment of what was spiritually begun. Sarki (by the flesh) here denotes human effort and achievement as the mode of completion, not moral evil — the problem is category, not character: seeking to perfect by human means what God began by grace.
4: τοσαῦτα ἐπάθετε εἰκῇ (epathete eikē) — "did you experience so many things in vain?" Paschō can mean either "to experience" (neutral) or "to suffer" (specifically negative). Both senses probably operate here: the Galatians' experiences include blessings (gifts of the Spirit, v5) and probable social suffering (persecution from their own Jewish communities for embracing Paul's law-free gospel). To adopt circumcision and law-keeping now would retroactively declare all of it meaningless — they suffered for nothing if law-keeping was the essential thing they were missing.
5: ὁ οὖν ἐπιχορηγῶν ὑμῖν τὸ πνεῦμα καὶ ἐνεργῶν δυνάμεις ἐν ὑμῖν (epichorēgōn) — "the one who supplies you the Spirit and works miracles among you." Epichorēgeō (to supply bountifully, provide at one's own expense) derives from the Athenian practice of the wealthy citizen (chorēgos) who funded the theatrical chorus; the image is of lavish patron-scale provision. God supplies the Spirit not grudgingly but with patron-like abundance. Energōn dynameis (working miracles, operating powerful acts) — the present participle describes ongoing miraculous activity in the Galatian communities at the time of writing.
6: Ἀβραὰμ ἐπίστευσεν τῷ θεῷ καὶ ἐλογίσθη αὐτῷ εἰς δικαιοσύνην — citing Gen 15:6 (the same text as Rom 4:3). The aorist episteusen (believed) points to the specific act of trusting the divine promise of Gen 15:5 (offspring as numerous as the stars). The aorist passive elogisthē (was credited, was reckoned) — God's forensic act in response: a reckoning of righteousness based on faith, not on circumcision (which came in Gen 17, after the faith-reckoning). Paul's argument in ch 3 deploys Gen 15:6 as the foundational proof that the Abrahamic principle was always faith, since the law came 430 years after Abraham (v17).
7: οἱ ἐκ πίστεως, οὗτοί εἰσιν υἱοὶ Ἀβραάμ — "those who are of faith are the sons of Abraham." Hoi ek pisteōs (the faith-people, those who derive their standing from faith) is Paul's compact counter-definition of Abraham's true heirs: not those from ethnic descent or circumcision-lineage but those from the same faith-principle by which Abraham himself was reckoned righteous. The hermeneutical move redefines the people of God without denying the Abrahamic covenant — it claims the covenant's deepest logic against those who read it as an ethnic entailment.
8: προϊδοῦσα δὲ ἡ γραφὴ... προευηγγελίσατο τῷ Ἀβραάμ (proeuangelizomai) — "Scripture, foreseeing... proclaimed the gospel in advance to Abraham." Proeuangelizomai (to pre-proclaim the gospel, announce the good news beforehand) — a verb Paul appears to have coined (or uses uniquely in this form); the gospel is not a NT invention but the fulfillment of a pre-proclamation already embedded in the Genesis text. The personification proiidousa hē graphē (Scripture foreseeing) presents the OT text as a divinely-sighted agent: Gen 12:3 ("all nations will be blessed through you") was foresight of the Gentile mission, not merely ethnic promise.
9: ὥστε οἱ ἐκ πίστεως εὐλογοῦνται σὺν τῷ πιστῷ Ἀβραάμ — "so then, those who rely on faith are blessed along with Abraham, the man of faith." Syn (together with, alongside) — the Gentile faith-people enter the Abrahamic blessing-stream not as separate recipients of a different promise but as sharers in the same blessing in which Abraham stands. Pistos Abraam (believing / faithful Abraham) — the epithet identifies Abraham by his defining characteristic: his trust. The Gentile believers share both the mechanism (faith) and the destination (blessing) with their Abrahamic prototype.
10: ἐπικατάρατος πᾶς ὃς οὐκ ἐμμένει πᾶσιν τοῖς γεγραμμένοις ἐν τῷ βιβλίῳ τοῦ νόμου — citing Deut 27:26. The curse requires doing everything (pasin) written in the law — partial compliance is still non-compliance. Emmenō (to remain in, continue in, abide by) targets the sustained, ongoing character of what the law demands: not a single act of obedience but uninterrupted, total compliance. The curse is therefore comprehensive: anyone who enters the law's framework takes on an obligation no human being has ever met, and falls under the curse the moment the perfect compliance breaks down.
11: ὅτι δὲ ἐν νόμῳ οὐδεὶς δικαιοῦται παρὰ τῷ θεῷ δῆλον — "that no one is justified before God by the law is clear." Dēlon (clear, evident, self-evident) — Paul treats this as derivable from Scripture itself, not merely from his argument. The proof is Hab 2:4: "the righteous will live by faith" — if faith is the principle of the righteous person's life, law-performance cannot be that principle. The two texts (Deut 27:26 and Hab 2:4) bracket the discussion: the law brings curse; the prophets establish faith as the source of life.
12: ὁ δὲ νόμος οὐκ ἔστιν ἐκ πίστεως — "but the law is not from faith." The law and faith operate by different and logically exclusive principles: the law demands doing (poiēsas auta zēsetai = "the one who does them will live," citing Lev 18:5); faith receives as gift what it cannot produce. The two systems cannot simultaneously function as the mechanism of justification — one cannot both receive righteousness as a gift and earn it as a wage (cf. Rom 4:4-5). The law is not wrong but it is categorically different from faith.
13: Χριστὸς ἡμᾶς ἐξηγόρασεν ἐκ τῆς κατάρας τοῦ νόμου γενόμενος ὑπὲρ ἡμῶν κατάρα — "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us." Exagorazō (to buy out of, ransom from the slave market) — the redemption image: the law's curse had a price, and Christ paid it by bearing it himself. The opening of this verse has no connecting particle in Greek, giving it a staccato sharpness — a deliberate stylistic move that makes the statement land without diplomatic preparation. Genomenos hyper hēmōn katara (having become a curse on our behalf) — Paul says Christ actually became the curse itself, not merely that he suffered its effects: the one who bore it became what it was. The citation of Deut 21:23 (epikataratos pas ho kremamenos epi xylou = cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree) connects Roman crucifixion to the Deuteronomic curse-structure: the cross was not merely a method of execution but the form of the covenant curse.
14: ἵνα εἰς τὰ ἔθνη ἡ εὐλογία τοῦ Ἀβραὰμ γένηται ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ — "so that the blessing of Abraham might come to the Gentiles through Christ Jesus." The double hina (purpose clauses) reveals that Christ's curse-bearing has two goals: (1) the Abrahamic blessing reaches the Gentiles, and (2) the promised Spirit is received through faith. The Spirit is identified as the content of the Abrahamic blessing in its new-covenant form: what the promise to Abraham ultimately delivers is not merely territory or national descendants but the Spirit who creates the new-covenant family across all nations.
15: κατὰ ἄνθρωπον λέγω — "I am speaking in human terms." The shift to an everyday legal analogy (kata anthrōpon = according to human practice) grounds the theological argument in accessible civil experience before the audience argues it has no relevance to divine dealings. ὅμως ἀνθρώπου κεκυρωμένην διαθήκην οὐδεὶς ἀθετεῖ ἢ ἐπιδιατάσσεται — "no one annuls or adds to a ratified human will/covenant." Kekyrōmenēn diathēkēn (ratified covenant, confirmed legal document) — the perfect participle indicates the permanent validity of the ratification; a sealed covenant cannot be unilaterally altered by one party under both Greek and Roman law. The a fortiori argument is unstated but obvious: if humans honor their ratified covenants, how much more must God's ratified promise stand inviolate.
16: τῷ δὲ Ἀβραὰμ ἐρρέθησαν αἱ ἐπαγγελίαι καὶ τῷ σπέρματι αὐτοῦ — "the promises were spoken to Abraham and to his seed." οὐ λέγει· καὶ τοῖς σπέρμασιν... ἀλλ᾽ ὡς ἐφ᾽ ἑνός· καὶ τῷ σπέρματί σου, ὅς ἐστιν Χριστός — "it does not say 'seeds,' referring to many, but 'your seed,' referring to one, who is Christ." Paul's exegesis of the singular sperma (seed) in Genesis deploys a rabbinic method: noticing the grammatical number of a word and drawing theological inference from it. This was a recognized interpretive technique in Second Temple Judaism. The argument identifies the ultimate referent of the Abrahamic promise not as a collective ethnic lineage but as the singular Seed-who-is-Christ — making the promise fundamentally messianic, not merely genealogical.
17: ὁ μετὰ τετρακόσια καὶ τριάκοντα ἔτη γεγονὼς νόμος οὐκ ἀκυροῖ — "the law, which came four hundred and thirty years later, does not annul the covenant." The 430 years from Exod 12:40 (the length of the Egyptian sojourn) establishes the chronological priority of the Abrahamic promise over the Sinaitic law: promise first, law second — and the later cannot retroactively invalidate the prior. Akuroō (to deprive of authority, invalidate, cancel) — the precise legal term for nullifying a prior legal instrument; the covenant-promise is not only prior but immune to retrospective annulment by any subsequent decree.
18: εἰ γὰρ ἐκ νόμου ἡ κληρονομία, οὐκέτι ἐξ ἐπαγγελίας — "if the inheritance is from the law, it is no longer from the promise." The two principles — law and promise — are mutually exclusive as the basis of inheritance; if one operates, the other cannot. τῷ δὲ Ἀβραὰμ δι᾽ ἐπαγγελίας κεκχάρισται ὁ θεός — "God graciously gave it to Abraham through a promise." Kecharistamai (perfect of charizomai) — the gift was given as an act of grace and the giving stands permanently: the perfect tense marks the irrevocability. Inheritance-by-law and inheritance-by-gift are not two versions of the same thing; they are structurally opposed mechanisms.
19: ὁ νόμος τῶν παραβάσεων χάριν προσετέθη — "the law was added because of transgressions." Prostithēmi (to add alongside, add in addition to something already existing) positions the law as supplementary, not foundational: it was added to the already-existing covenant-promise structure. Tōn parabaseōn charin (for the sake of transgressions) — the law's function in relation to sin is to define it as transgression, multiply it, and make it fully visible (cf. Rom 5:20; 7:7-8). ἄχρις οὗ ἔλθῃ τὸ σπέρμα — "until the Seed came" — the law had an explicitly temporary function, bounded by the arrival of Christ the Seed. διαταγεὶς δι᾽ ἀγγέλων ἐν χειρὶ μεσίτου — "ordained through angels by a mediator" — a Second Temple tradition (Deut 33:2 LXX; Acts 7:53; Heb 2:2) that the law was delivered at Sinai through the mediation of angels and Moses; the mediated, two-party character of the law contrasts with the directness of the divine promise given by God alone.
20: ὁ δὲ μεσίτης ἑνὸς οὐκ ἔστιν, ὁ δὲ θεὸς εἷς ἐστιν — "a mediator is not for one party alone, but God is one." The argument: mediation requires multiple parties (at minimum two) who must both perform their obligations. The promise, by contrast, was a unilateral divine declaration: God alone was the speaking party; no human response or compliance was required to make the promise valid. The law needed Moses as mediator and required Israel's obedience as the condition; the promise needed neither. Its validity rests entirely on God's own faithfulness, and God is one — undivided, unchanging, requiring no second party to ratify what he has declared.
21: ὁ οὖν νόμος κατὰ τῶν ἐπαγγελιῶν τοῦ θεοῦ; μὴ γένοιτο — "is the law then opposed to the promises of God? By no means!" The anticipated objection: if the law is temporary, supplementary, and unable to justify, does this make it God's enemy? Paul's answer is no — but the reason reveals the law's real scope: εἰ γὰρ ἐδόθη νόμος ὁ δυνάμενος ζωοποιῆσαι, ὄντως ἐκ νόμου ἂν ἦν ἡ δικαιοσύνη — "for if a law had been given that was capable of giving life, righteousness would certainly have come from the law." The contrary-to-fact conditional is decisive: no such life-giving law was given. Law and promise operate in different domains — the law defines sin and cannot produce life; the promise gives what the law requires and cannot supply.
22: ἀλλὰ συνέκλεισεν ἡ γραφὴ τὰ πάντα ὑπὸ ἁμαρτίαν (synekleis en) — "but Scripture locked up everything under sin." Synkleiō (to close in from all sides, hem in completely, shut up in an enclosure) — the image is of a shepherd closing his flock in a fold with no exit, or a net drawn tight around fish: the confinement is complete and there is no way out through one's own effort. Everything (ta panta) is enclosed — no exception for the especially devout. ἵνα ἡ ἐπαγγελία ἐκ πίστεως Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ δοθῇ τοῖς πιστεύουσιν — the purpose of the confinement is redemptive: the enclosure drives all toward the single available exit — the promise-gift given through faith in Christ.
23: πρὸ τοῦ δὲ ἐλθεῖν τὴν πίστιν ὑπὸ νόμον ἐφρουρούμεθα συγκεκλεισμένοι — "before this faith came, we were held in custody under the law, locked in." Phrouroumetha (imperfect passive: we were being kept under guard) — the military image of garrison troops keeping a population in custody; the law's role was that of a jailer maintaining a state of confinement. Sygkekleismenoi (locked in together, doubly enclosed) — the compound intensifies the image of total confinement. Crucially, the custody was not punitive for its own sake but prospective: eis tēn mellousan pistin apokalyphthēnai — "in anticipation of the faith that was about to be revealed." The lock-up was a holding pattern, not a permanent sentence.
24: ὥστε ὁ νόμος παιδαγωγὸς ἡμῶν γέγονεν εἰς Χριστόν — "so then the law was our guardian until Christ." Paidagōgos is not the teacher but the household slave who escorted a minor child to school, maintained discipline en route, and ensured the child arrived safely. The paidagōgos' function was temporary by definition — it ended when the child reached majority. Paul's metaphor is precise: the law's role was custodial and preparatory, not pedagogical in the permanent or instructive sense. Its task was to escort Israel to Christ, the destination; once arrived, the escort's function is complete. ἵνα ἐκ πίστεως δικαιωθῶμεν — "so that we might be justified by faith" — the purpose of the preparatory guardianship: leading to the justification-by-faith that the Christ-event makes possible and available.
25: ἐλθούσης δὲ τῆς πίστεως οὐκέτι ὑπὸ παιδαγωγόν ἐσμεν — "but now that this faith has come, we are no longer under a guardian." The aorist participle elthousēs (having come) marks the arrival of "faith" as a dateable historical event — the coming of Christ and the proclamation of his gospel. "No longer under a guardian" is the declaration of covenant maturity: the law's custodial role has ended for all who are in Christ. The Galatians who seek to take on the law's observances are going backward — seeking to return to minority status after the arrival of majority, to return to the escort-slave's hand after reaching the school.
26: πάντες γὰρ υἱοὶ θεοῦ ἐστε διὰ τῆς πίστεως ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ — "for you are all sons of God through faith in Christ Jesus." Pantes (all) — the universal scope is the point: all believers, without ethnic or social qualification, are sons/children of God. The status of covenant sonship that was previously the privilege of Israel-as-nation (Exod 4:22; Deut 14:1) is now available to all through union with the Son. Dia tēs pisteōs en Christō (through faith, in Christ) — the mechanism (faith) and the sphere (in Christ) are inseparable: sonship is not simply a new status assigned from outside but a relation entered by union with the one who is Son.
27: ὅσοι γὰρ εἰς Χριστὸν ἐβαπτίσθητε, Χριστὸν ἐνεδύσασθε — "for all of you who were baptized into Christ have clothed yourselves with Christ." Eis Christon ebaptisthēte (were baptized into Christ) — the preposition eis (into) marks entry into a sphere, a relation, a new domain; baptism is the rite of incorporation, not merely a rite of initiation. Enedysasthe (aorist middle: you clothed yourselves, you put on) — the baptismal clothing image: the physical act of removing garments before the water and being vested in white afterward enacted the theological reality of putting on Christ as a new identity-garment. The putting-on is not external costume but a change in whose life defines your life.
28:
οὐκ ἔνι Ἰουδαῖος οὐδὲ Ἕλλην, οὐκ ἔνι δοῦλος οὐδὲ ἐλεύθερος, οὐκ ἔνι ἄρσεν καὶ θῆλυ — "there is neither Jew nor Greek, neither slave nor free, nor is there male and female." The three pairs cover the primary structural divisions of the ancient world: ethnic (Jew/Greek), socio-economic (slave/free), and biological (male/female). Note that the third pair shifts from oude... oude (neither... nor) to kai (and) — arsen kai thēly — possibly echoing the creation formula of Gen 1:27 ("male and female he created them"), suggesting that in Christ even the fundamental creaturely distinction is relativized in terms of standing before God.

Ouk eni (there is not, it does not exist) — an emphatic contracted form: these categories do not function as determinants of standing in Christ. They are not abolished as real distinctions; they do not determine one's access to God or one's spiritual status within the community.
29: εἰ δὲ ὑμεῖς Χριστοῦ, ἄρα τοῦ Ἀβραὰμ σπέρμα ἐστέ, κατ᾽ ἐπαγγελίαν κληρονόμοι — "if you belong to Christ, then you are Abraham's seed, and heirs according to the promise." The syllogism completing the argument of ch 3: Christ is the Seed of Abraham (v16); those who belong to Christ share his identity; therefore they are Abraham's seed and heirs. The inheritance is kat' epangelian (according to promise) — not according to ethnic descent, not according to circumcision-status, not according to law-observance, but according to the original mechanism by which Abraham himself was reckoned righteous. The Gentile Galatians who are in Christ are Abraham's heirs in the fullest sense, because they stand on the same faith-ground on which Abraham stood before any of the national markers existed.
